name: Startup Pitch Deck
summary: The canonical investor-pitch arc, drawn from Sequoia and Y Combinator's templates. Optimized for 10–20 minute meetings with VCs and angels. Each beat answers the question an investor will ask next.

beats.name: Opening
beats.description: Open with the company name, logo, and a one-line tagline that doubles as a positioning statement. Set the visual tone for the deck. Keep it confident and uncluttered — this is the cover.

beats.name: Company Purpose
beats.description: State in a single sentence what the company does and why it exists. Should be immediately understandable without industry jargon. Often doubles as the answer to 'so what do you do?'

beats.name: Problem
beats.description: Describe the pain point you solve. Make it concrete with a specific user or customer scenario. The more visceral and recognizable the problem, the stronger the rest of the pitch lands.

beats.name: Why Now
beats.description: Explain the technological, regulatory, behavioral, or market shift that makes this opportunity possible today and impossible five years ago. Investors want to know why this is a window, not just an idea.

beats.name: Market Size
beats.description: Size the opportunity. Top-down (TAM/SAM/SOM) or bottom-up (units × price). Show how big the prize is if you win, and why this market is attractive to a venture-scale outcome.

beats.name: Competition
beats.description: Map the competitive landscape honestly. A 2x2 matrix or feature-comparison grid is fine; what matters is showing you understand the alternatives and where you uniquely fit. Naïve 'no competition' answers tank credibility.

beats.name: Solution
beats.description: Show how you solve the problem and why your approach is uniquely better. Lead with the outcome for the customer, then show the mechanism. This is where you connect problem to product.

beats.name: Product
beats.description: Give the product its own moment. Screenshots or a key visual that captures what users actually see and feel. Pick one strong frame over a montage of small ones.

beats.name: Demo
beats.description: Walk through the core user flow end-to-end. Show real software solving a real problem in a real workflow — not a marketing reel. If a live demo is too risky, use a tightly edited recording with narration.

beats.name: Traction
beats.description: Prove momentum with one credible headline metric. Revenue, users, growth rate, customer logos — whatever best shows the business is working. One strong chart beats five vanity numbers.

beats.name: Growth Drivers
beats.description: Show how the headline metric got there and where it's going. Cohorts, retention, expansion, channel mix — whatever explains the engine behind the number. Investors back the trajectory, not the snapshot.

beats.name: Business Model
beats.description: Explain how the company makes money. Pricing, unit economics, sales motion, payback period. Should answer: what does it cost to acquire a customer, and what do they pay you?

beats.name: Team
beats.description: Establish why this team uniquely wins this market. Founder backgrounds, relevant experience, prior outcomes, key advisors. Investors back people first; make the case.

beats.name: Financials
beats.description: Show the financial picture: historical revenue, burn, runway, and forward projection. Be conservative-but-credible — investors will discount aggressive numbers and reward defensible ones.

beats.name: The Ask
beats.description: State the round size, intended use of funds, and what milestones the capital takes you to. Be specific — investors should leave knowing exactly what you want and what they get.

beats.name: Closing
beats.description: Close on a strong final image: logo, contact info, and the one line you most want the investor to remember. Leave space for the conversation that follows.
